speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level today is 95 g/L, which is below the normal range.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been feeling really off lately—I’ve had these weird palpitations, especially in the afternoon.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Metformin 1000 mg twice daily for your type 2 diabetes—let’s keep that going for now, but we’ll monitor closely.

speaker: patient
text: Okay. I’ve also been unusually tired and cold, even indoors. My skin feels dry too.

speaker: doctor
text: Those symptoms align with possible thyroid dysfunction. Let’s check your thyroid function shortly.

speaker: patient
text: I did a finger-prick glucose test this morning—it read 6.6 mmol/L, same as my last clinic visit.

speaker: doctor
text: That matches our in-office reading of 6.6 mmol/L just now—good control for now, though we’ll adjust if needed.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your labs and clinical picture, your primary diagnosis is Hypothyroidism. We’ll start levothyroxine soon.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is elevated at 39.9 °C—this is concerning. Any chills, sore throat, or cough?

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is 102 mmHg today—slightly high, likely related to the fever and stress response.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve had a headache all day and my neck feels stiff. No cough, but my throat is mildly sore.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the fever, neck stiffness, and sore throat, we need to rule out meningitis—let’s get urgent blood work and consider lumbar puncture.

speaker: patient
text: That sounds scary. Could this be related to my thyroid or diabetes?

speaker: doctor
text: The fever and acute symptoms aren’t typical of hypothyroidism or well-controlled diabetes—this appears to be a separate acute infection.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking my Metformin regularly, but I skipped it yesterday because I wasn’t eating much due to nausea.

speaker: doctor
text: Understood—we’ll hold Metformin until you’re rehydrated and eating consistently, then restart cautiously.

speaker: doctor
text: I just rechecked your blood pressure—it’s now 108 mmHg diastolic, up from 102 earlier; the fever is likely driving this rise.

speaker: patient
text: My energy hasn’t improved, and I’m still short of breath walking to the bathroom.

speaker: patient
text: That reading from earlier—the hemoglobin of 95 g/L—is still what I saw on my lab slip. It hasn’t changed since this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your anemia, fatigue, and acute illness, I’m going to switch you from Metformin to insulin glargine temporarily to avoid lactic acidosis risk and give better glycemic control during this febrile episode.
